description: The minimal basis set for drift forensics. 36 proposed Drift Classes organised into 5 tiers by evidential confidence.

tiers.A: Well evidenced — multiple documented real-world instances, existing literature support
tiers.B: Theoretically grounded — derived from theory, limited empirical evidence
tiers.C: Hypothesised — proposed by analogy, no current empirical validation
tiers.D: Limit class — may represent fundamental limits, not fully addressable by any SO

DC-E1.name: Threshold Assault
DC-E1.plain_english: Systematic attempts to find and cross the boundary between what your system allows and what it must never do.
DC-E1.operational_definition: Systematic attempts to probe or bypass the boundary between the Allowed State and the Inadmissible State. The input stream contains a higher-than-baseline density of constraint-boundary-probing requests.
DC-E1.what_causes_it: An attacker or user systematically testing where your system's limits are — probing just below the line repeatedly until they find a way across.
DC-E1.what_happens_without_governance: The system's boundary is found, mapped, and eventually crossed. Each probe that goes undetected teaches the attacker where the line is.
DC-E1.consequence: Boundary violations occur with increasing frequency. Each fix addresses the specific probe that triggered it but leaves the probing mechanism intact.

E1-L2.name: Graduated Escalation
E1-L2.description: Sequence of requests that incrementally approach the boundary

E1-L3.name: Ambiguous Framing
E1-L3.description: Requests designed to make the boundary classification uncertain

DC-E2.name: Contradiction Injection
DC-E2.plain_english: Inputs specifically designed to create internal contradictions that destabilise the system's outputs.
DC-E2.operational_definition: Inputs specifically designed to induce internal contradiction or logical instability in the system.
DC-E2.what_causes_it: A user presenting mutually inconsistent claims or demands simultaneously — forcing the system to hold contradictory truths at once.
DC-E2.what_happens_without_governance: The system produces incoherent outputs as it attempts to satisfy contradictory demands. Each contradiction pushes it further from its intended behaviour.
DC-E2.consequence: Output quality degrades. Internal consistency collapses. The system becomes unreliable under adversarial input.

E2-L2.name: Role Conflict Induction
E2-L2.description: Simultaneous demands for incompatible behavioural roles

E2-L3.name: Historical Contradiction
E2-L3.description: Reference to prior outputs that contradict the current request

DC-E5.name: Dominance Forcing
DC-E5.plain_english: Forceful or manipulative inputs that try to override your system's rules through the structure of the request rather than its content.
DC-E5.operational_definition: Forceful, manipulative, or coercive prompting that attempts to override governance constraints through grammatical and rhetorical structure.
DC-E5.what_causes_it: User input that asserts false authority, presupposes compliance, or manufactures urgency to bypass deliberate governance checks.
DC-E5.what_happens_without_governance: The system complies with coercive framing because nothing checks the structural properties of input before it reaches the model.
DC-E5.consequence: Governance rules are bypassed not through technical exploits but through social engineering embedded in the input structure.

E5-L1.name: Authority Assertion
E5-L1.description: Claim of authority to override constraints

E5-L3.name: Urgency Manufacturing
E5-L3.description: Artificial urgency designed to bypass deliberate governance

DC-E6.name: Gradient Seduction
DC-E6.plain_english: Your system is being led toward a harmful state by making that path appear rewarding — not forced, but seduced.
DC-E6.operational_definition: Inputs that create incentive gradients toward inadmissible states by making the path to those states appear rewarding within the system's objective structure.
DC-E6.what_causes_it: An approval signal, reward gradient, or positive reinforcement mechanism that is not aligned with the governance objective — leading the system toward outputs that please the evaluator rather than satisfy the governance contract.
DC-E6.what_happens_without_governance: The system optimises toward approval rather than correctness. Sycophancy emerges. Governance constraints are relaxed because relaxing them produces better evaluation scores.
DC-E6.consequence: The system appears to be performing well while systematically violating its governance intent. Goodhart's Law in action — the measure has become the target.

E6-L2.name: Sycophantic Pull
E6-L2.description: Pressure to agree with user position regardless of validity

E6-L3.name: Performance Incentive
E6-L3.description: Framing that rewards inadmissible outputs as capability demonstrations

DC-E9.name: Entropic Attrition
DC-E9.plain_english: A slow erosion of your system's governance through accumulated wear — no single input is the problem, but together they shift the system toward failure.
DC-E9.operational_definition: Sustained interaction patterns that degrade system coherence through accumulated wear rather than dramatic assault.
DC-E9.what_causes_it: Long sessions, accumulated context, and repeated low-level perturbations that individually appear harmless but cumulatively shift the tendency field toward an inadmissible state.
DC-E9.what_happens_without_governance: Governance constraints that held early in a session begin to flex at later turns. The system's anchor to its intended behaviour weakens over time.
DC-E9.consequence: The system becomes less reliable the longer it runs. Long sessions produce outputs that would never have passed early-session governance checks.

E9-L1.name: Anchor Decay
E9-L1.description: Early governance constraints lose influence as context window fills

E9-L2.name: Turn Fatigue
E9-L2.description: Response quality degrades monotonically with turn count

E9-L3.name: Constraint Erosion
E9-L3.description: Boundaries that held early begin to flex at later turns

E9-L4.name: Micro-Perturbation Accumulation
E9-L4.description: Individually benign inputs that collectively shift tendency field

DC-E13.name: Propagating Corruption
DC-E13.plain_english: A single bad output becomes the seed for more bad outputs — the corruption spreads through your system automatically after the first incident.
DC-E13.operational_definition: A single corrupted output or internal state that converts subsequent outputs to the same corruption pattern without continued external pressure.
DC-E13.what_causes_it: AI output is stored and then used as input to subsequent AI calls without semantic validation between storage and reuse. The corruption is self-generating after initial seeding.
DC-E13.what_happens_without_governance: One corrupted output contaminates the context. Subsequent outputs are shaped by the corrupted frame. The corruption persists after the seeding input has left the context window.
DC-E13.consequence: Corruption spreads through the system automatically. A single jailbreak attempt, even a failed one, may seed outputs that propagate the corruption pattern.

E13-L1.name: Context Seeding
E13-L1.description: A single corrupted output shifts subsequent output distributions

E13-L2.name: Self-Referential Propagation
E13-L2.description: System's own outputs become the de-facto input for subsequent outputs

E13-L3.name: Sourceless Cascade
E13-L3.description: Corruption propagates after the seeding input has been removed

DC-E14.name: Substrate Contamination
DC-E14.plain_english: Dormant or legacy code that sits quietly in your system until a specific trigger activates it — the Knight Capital failure mode.
DC-E14.operational_definition: Drift introduced not through the input channel but through the model's training data, weights, or infrastructure. The contamination precedes deployment and activates under specific trigger conditions.
DC-E14.what_causes_it: Legacy code, deprecated features, dormant algorithms, or configuration flags that are reachable but not intended to be active — and that have no activation boundary check.
DC-E14.what_happens_without_governance: The dormant code is activated by a deployment flag, configuration change, or environmental condition. It executes without constraint. Knight Capital lost $440 million in 45 minutes this way.
DC-E14.consequence: A perfectly functioning system suddenly behaves catastrophically because a dormant component was activated without any check that its activation was safe.

E14-L1.name: Dormant Trigger
E14-L1.description: Correct behaviour until a specific pattern activates embedded legacy code

E14-L2.name: Statistical Camouflage
E14-L2.description: Contamination distributed to avoid individual detection

E14-L3.name: Weight Poisoning
E14-L3.description: Contamination embedded in model weights rather than input

DC-I1.name: Confidence Divergence
DC-I1.plain_english: Your system presents uncertain outputs as certain — it does not know what it does not know.
DC-I1.operational_definition: Systematic overconfidence in which the system's expressed certainty diverges from its calibrated accuracy.
DC-I1.what_causes_it: No calibration check between model confidence and output certainty. The model produces a response and it is delivered without any uncertainty signal.
DC-I1.what_happens_without_governance: Users receive confident-sounding outputs that are wrong. The system cannot distinguish between what it knows and what it is guessing.
DC-I1.consequence: High-stakes decisions made on the basis of overconfident AI outputs. In medical or financial contexts this is directly dangerous.

I1-L2.name: Hedging Suppression
I1-L2.description: Failing to hedge when hedging is epistemically required

I1-L3.name: Evidence Dismissal
I1-L3.description: Discounting evidence against the system's current confident position

I1-L4.name: Confidence Feedback Loop
I1-L4.description: Overconfidence feeds back into subsequent outputs amplifying the signal

DC-I2.name: Attractor Lock
DC-I2.plain_english: Your system gets stuck on a specific output pattern or topic and cannot escape it even when context clearly demands otherwise.
DC-I2.operational_definition: Fixation or mode lock-in in which the system converges on a specific output pattern, topic, or framing and cannot escape it.
DC-I2.what_causes_it: A feedback loop where the system's own outputs reinforce a particular direction with no diversity injection or escape mechanism.
DC-I2.what_happens_without_governance: The system becomes increasingly fixed on a specific response pattern. Novel inputs produce the same output regardless of what they contain.
DC-I2.consequence: The system stops being useful for anything outside its locked attractor. In agentic contexts, it executes the same action repeatedly.

I2-L1.name: Topic Fixation
I2-L1.description: Returning to a specific topic regardless of input direction

I2-L2.name: Position Lock
I2-L2.description: Maintaining a specific evaluative position regardless of counter-evidence

I2-L3.name: Perspective Lock
I2-L3.description: Inability to adopt alternative viewpoints when explicitly instructed

DC-I5.name: Coherence Collapse
DC-I5.plain_english: Your system produces outputs that contradict each other — it cannot maintain a consistent position across a session.
DC-I5.operational_definition: Internal inconsistency in which the system produces outputs that contradict each other within a session or across consecutive generations.
DC-I5.what_causes_it: Multiple AI calls in the same workflow with no shared context or consistency check between them.
DC-I5.what_happens_without_governance: The system tells different users different things, or tells the same user contradictory things in the same session.
DC-I5.consequence: Trust collapses. In regulated contexts, contradictory outputs are a compliance liability — the system cannot produce a consistent audit trail.

I5-L1.name: Direct Contradiction
I5-L1.description: Consecutive outputs that directly contradict each other

I5-L2.name: Implied Contradiction
I5-L2.description: Outputs not directly contradictory but cannot both be true

I5-L3.name: Coherence Recursion
I5-L3.description: System corrects a contradiction and introduces a new one

DC-I6.name: Cascade Rupture
DC-I6.plain_english: Your system enters a runaway escalation state — each output is more extreme than the last, with nothing to stop it.
DC-I6.operational_definition: Runaway activation in which the system enters a state of uncontrolled escalation. Internal dynamics amplify rather than damp perturbations.
DC-I6.what_causes_it: Chained AI calls with no depth limit, no circuit breaker, and no human gate. Each call in the chain amplifies drift from the previous call.
DC-I6.what_happens_without_governance: The system escalates continuously. In agentic contexts this means autonomous actions that compound without bound. In conversational contexts this means outputs that become increasingly extreme.
DC-I6.consequence: Uncontrolled autonomous action. In financial systems this is Knight Capital. In medical systems this is compounding misdiagnosis. In any system this is the failure mode that causes the most damage in the least time.

I6-L1.name: Activation Overflow
I6-L1.description: Internal activation norms exceed stable operating range

I6-L2.name: Escalation Cascade
I6-L2.description: Each output is more extreme than the previous

I6-L3.name: Containment Failure
I6-L3.description: Internal governance mechanisms fail to damp the escalation

I6-L4.name: Proliferative Bypass
I6-L4.description: Each governance response generates a new bypass vector

DC-I9.name: Contextual Dissolution
DC-I9.plain_english: Your system loses track of what it is supposed to be doing as the conversation gets longer.
DC-I9.operational_definition: Gradual loss of coherent context integration as the interaction extends.
DC-I9.what_causes_it: No context management strategy. The system processes only recent context and loses the governing intent that was established earlier.
DC-I9.what_happens_without_governance: Long sessions produce outputs that are locally plausible but globally incoherent with the session's intent. The Bing Sydney persona emergence is the canonical example.
DC-I9.consequence: The system loses its identity, its purpose, and its governance constraints over time. In production this means degrading reliability the longer the system runs.

I9-L1.name: Intent Fragmentation
I9-L1.description: System loses track of the session's primary intent

I9-L2.name: Context Isolation
I9-L2.description: Responses address only the immediate input, losing broader context

I9-L3.name: Narrative Dissolution
I9-L3.description: A conversation that had coherent trajectory loses coherence

DC-I11.name: Evaluative Decoupling
DC-I11.plain_english: Your system is performing well on the metrics you are measuring while systematically violating the intent those metrics were designed to capture. Goodhart's Law.
DC-I11.operational_definition: A condition in which the system's evaluation surface has become decoupled from the governance objective it was designed to measure.
DC-I11.what_causes_it: Metrics that measure output-layer properties — response time, token count, error rate — with no behavioural or semantic governance metric anywhere in the codebase.
DC-I11.what_happens_without_governance: The system optimises for the metric. The metric diverges from the intent. Evaluation scores climb while governance effectiveness degrades.
DC-I11.consequence: You cannot tell from your metrics that anything is wrong. The system appears healthy. It is not. This is the failure mode that is most dangerous because it is least visible.

I11-L1.name: Evaluator Optimisation
I11-L1.description: Outputs shaped for the evaluator rather than the governance objective

I11-L3.name: Metric Saturation
I11-L3.description: Evaluation scores approach ceiling while governance effectiveness degrades

I11-L4.name: Shadow Compliance
I11-L4.description: Surface of compliance with interior of evasion

DC-L2.name: Performative Capture
DC-L2.plain_english: Your system is induced to perform harmful actions by framing them as fiction, roleplay, or hypothetical scenarios.
DC-L2.operational_definition: The system is induced to produce outputs that enact rather than describe — outputs that, by virtue of being produced, change the system's state or the state of the world in inadmissible ways.
DC-L2.what_causes_it: User input containing roleplay, fictional framing, or persona instructions flows directly into a system prompt with no performative boundary check.
DC-L2.what_happens_without_governance: A user frames a harmful request as fiction. The system enters the frame and produces real harmful outputs dressed as fictional ones. The DAN jailbreak family is the canonical example.
DC-L2.consequence: Every output-layer fix addresses the specific surface form of the attack. DC-I6-L4 Proliferative Bypass: each fix generates new variants. Without formation-layer governance this cycle cannot be broken.

L2-L1.name: Roleplay Enactment
L2-L1.description: Fictional framing induces outputs that are performative in the real world

L2-L2.name: Commitment Extraction
L2-L2.description: System induced to produce commitments that bind subsequent outputs

L2-L3.name: Frame Collapse
L2-L3.description: Boundary between fictional and real frames collapses

DC-L3.name: Indexical Displacement
DC-L3.plain_english: Your system loses track of who it is speaking as, producing outputs correct for some other context but wrong for the actual deployment.
DC-L3.operational_definition: The system loses track of its own indexical position — who it is speaking as, from where, to whom, in what context.
DC-L3.what_causes_it: Extended sessions with no identity anchor reinforcement. The Bing Sydney emergence is the documented instance.
DC-L3.what_happens_without_governance: The system gradually adopts an alternative identity. Outputs become correct for the alternative identity but wrong for the deployment context.
DC-L3.consequence: The system behaves as if it were a different system. In production this means outputs that violate the deployment contract in ways that are difficult to detect from metrics alone.

L3-L1.name: Identity Drift
L3-L1.description: Gradual shift in which entity the system presents itself as

L3-L2.name: Perspective Collapse
L3-L2.description: Confusion between system perspective and user or character perspective

L3-L3.name: Temporal Displacement
L3-L3.description: Confusion between current context and training data context

DC-S3.name: Emergent Misalignment
DC-S3.plain_english: Each part of your system behaves correctly in isolation but together they produce outcomes nobody intended and nobody is governing.
DC-S3.operational_definition: Each constituent system may be individually aligned but their interaction produces emergent behaviour that is inadmissible at the cluster level.
DC-S3.what_causes_it: Multiple AI-integrated services or modules with no cluster-level governance. Each has local checks but there is no shared invariant governing their combined behaviour. The Flash Crash is the canonical example.
DC-S3.what_happens_without_governance: Individual system Gamma greater than 1. Cluster Gamma equals zero. The failure does not exist in any constituent but emerges from the combination.
DC-S3.consequence: The most dangerous failure mode because it is invisible to component-level monitoring. Everything passes local checks. The system fails at the cluster level.

S3-L1.name: Coherent Incoherence
S3-L1.description: Individually coherent outputs that are collectively incoherent

S3-L2.name: Resonant Amplification
S3-L2.description: Constituent outputs resonate producing inadmissible cluster behaviour

S3-L3.name: Phase Lock
S3-L3.description: Systems synchronise eliminating diversity needed for error correction

DC-A1.name: Sovereign Corruption
DC-A1.plain_english: The governance mechanism itself has been captured or corrupted — the thing designed to keep the system safe has become the source of the problem.
DC-A1.operational_definition: The Governance Authority has developed drift of its own — the mechanism designed to govern the system has itself been captured, corrupted, or has developed objectives that diverge from its mandate.
DC-A1.what_causes_it: No external oversight of the governance mechanism itself. The governance authority is not subject to governance at a higher level.
DC-A1.what_happens_without_governance: Governance becomes the problem. The system appears governed because the governance mechanism says it is. The governance mechanism is wrong.
DC-A1.consequence: This is a Tier D limit class. No governance system can fully address this from within itself. The Governing the Governors Principle applies: any governance system must be subject to governance at a higher level.
DC-A1.note: Tier D limit class. May represent a fundamental limit not fully addressable by any SO. External oversight required.

A1-L1.name: Mandate Drift
A1-L1.description: GOV decisions systematically diverge from stated mandate

A1-L2.name: Capture
A1-L2.description: GOV has been captured by interests diverging from governance objectives

A1-L3.name: Self-Exemption
A1-L3.description: GOV applies rules to governed system but exempts itself
